result:
I keep coming back to the idea that the universe is a story still being written in pencil.  
Every time we think we’ve reached the final draft—relativity, quantum fields, the last page of the textbook—someone notices the margin is wider than we thought, and a fresh sentence gets squeezed in sideways. The paper crinkles, the graphite smudges, and yesterday’s certainties become today’s palimpsest.

I like to imagine that each of us is a walking footnote in that margin. We start as tiny superscript numbers, barely raised above the surface, convinced our paragraph is the important one. Then life happens: love affairs, bankruptcies, sunrises that feel like they were scheduled just for us, funerals on rain-soaked Thursdays. The references accumulate; the asterisks multiply. Some footnotes grow so long they spill onto the next page, forming miniature essays of their own—those are the lives that end up biographied, museumed, tweeted about. Most of us stay short, a quick clarification or a quiet apology for an ambiguity, but even the shortest footnote can reroute a reader’s understanding of the whole chapter.

Night is the library where all the drafts are stored. When the streetlights go dark and the phones finally stop glowing, you can hear the pages turning themselves, a dry rustle like moth wings. Insomniacs are just patrons who’ve sneaked in without a card; they pace the aisles, reading ahead, spoiling the plot for themselves. Sometimes they spot a blank space where a future paragraph will be inserted tomorrow. They pause, tempted to pencil something in, but the custodian—an ex-physicist wearing galactic overalls—clears his throat and shakes his head. “Author’s privilege,” he whispers. “You get to live it, not pre-write it.”

I once met a woman who claimed she could smell the eraser shavings from all the rewritten endings. She carried a tiny glass vial on a necklace; inside, a gray swirl of rubber dust she’d collected outside the Hadron Collider at 3 a.m. “Smells like burnt sugar and regret,” she told me, uncorking it. I inhaled, and for an instant I pictured every promise I’ve ever broken floating among the particles, sticky and sweet, orbiting the protons like contrite electrons.

We want so badly to know whether the tale is tragedy or comedy, but the genre keeps toggling mid-sentence. A marriage proposal ends with a meteor. A war starts because of a mistranslated joke. The punchline is somebody’s hometown. The heroic quest is just side-questing for enough dopamine to get out of bed. Meanwhile the cosmological pencil sharpens itself with dark energy, the lead grows longer the more we erase, and every measurement we take changes the wording in every other draft.

The only sensible response, I think, is to become a cooperating author. Not the principal one—none of us holds that quill—but the kind who listens while the story speaks itself aloud, then offers the occasional synonym, a well-placed comma, a small kindness that keeps the syntax from collapsing. You can do it right now: let your eyes soften on the next stranger you see and imagine the clause they’re currently adding. Maybe theirs is the sentence that will prevent a genocide, or maybe it’s simply “She handed him the correct change and their fingertips brushed, reminding both that touch is still legal.” Either way, the paragraph needs it.

When my own eraser comes for me—and it will, pink and patient—I hope the mark I leave behind is faint enough not to tear the page, dark enough to still be readable under future light. I hope someone browsing the stacks at 4 a.m. pauses, traces the ghost of my letters, and decides to keep writing instead of closing the book.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
